ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Singapore economic rice. A00LC (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible category anchor. Exact match unavailable.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Singapore economic rice or cai fan as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of economic rice with two vegetable dishes and one fish dish typically weighs approximately 350 g as served at a Singapore hawker centre or coffee shop.

nutrient_source.title: Economic Rice (2 Vegetables, 1 Fish) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per-100g nutrient fields. Values are accepted as sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry; set to -1 with documentation in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (cooked, Asian-style) GI ~72
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore economic rice (2 veg, 1 fish) as a composite dish. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component — cooked white rice (jasmine/long-grain, Asian preparation) — which is consistently reported at GI ~72 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The vegetable and fish components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 46.9) / 100 ≈ 33.8, rounded to 33.9. Serving carbohydrate estimated as 13.41 g/100g × 350 g serving = 46.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 206 mg/100g; approximately 721 mg per standard 350 g serving, approaching 30% of the 2400 mg daily limit
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and choose steamed or braised fish rather than fried preparations to keep the per-serving sodium below 600 mg; the potassium contributed by fish and vegetables provides a partial DASH-diet offset but does not fully neutralise the sodium load at typical serving sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice (GI 72) with 13.41 g carbohydrate per 100g produces a glycemic load of approximately 33.9 per standard serving, which is clinically high
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to half (approximately 100–120 g cooked rice) and increase the vegetable and fish components to lower the per-meal glycemic load below 20; substituting brown rice or requesting less rice are the most effective single interventions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is moderate at 3.0 g/100g; fish component provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids, but sodium at 206 mg/100g and refined-carbohydrate load are adverse factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease benefit from the omega-3 content of the fish component and should choose oily fish (mackerel, sardine) in steamed or braised preparation; they should request reduced soy sauce and a smaller rice portion to limit sodium and refined-carbohydrate intake in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fish provides omega-3 fatty acids with direct anti-inflammatory effect on joint tissue; two vegetable dishes supply antioxidants
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose oily fish (mackerel, sardine) in steamed or braised preparation to maximise omega-3 bioavailability and avoid AGE formation associated with deep-frying.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economic rice, known locally as cai fan (菜饭) or nasi ekonomi, is one of Singapore's most ubiquitous and affordable hawker-centre meals. A standard serving consists of a mound of steamed white rice topped with a self-selected combination of dishes from a display counter; this variant includes two vegetable dishes and one fish dish. The rice is typically jasmine or long-grain white rice, steamed plain. Vegetable dishes commonly include stir-fried leafy greens (kangkong, kai lan, chye sim), braised tofu, or egg dishes. The fish component may be steamed, braised in soy sauce, or deep-fried, with common choices including batang (Spanish mackerel), ikan kuning (yellow tail fusilier), or tilapia. Per 100 g the dish provides 102 kcal, 5.2 g protein, 3.0 g fat, 13.41 g carbohydrate, 1.2 g dietary fibre, and 206 mg sodium, as reported by the Health Promotion Board Singapore. The glycemic index is high (~72) driven by the white rice base, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The fish component contributes omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective properties. Sodium is borderline elevated and can be reduced by requesting less soy sauce. Economic rice is a culturally significant, budget-friendly, and nutritionally balanced meal when portion sizes are managed appropriately.

confidence_reason: All core macronutrient and secondary nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The food identity is exact. GI is assigned from the University of Sydney GI Database via analogous estimate for cooked white rice, which is the dominant carbohydrate contributor. Multilingual names are verified for Singaporean cuisine requirements.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Singapore economic rice dish are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this item as of 2026. The HPB source reports energy, protein, fat (total), carbohydrate, sugar, dietary fibre, sodium, calcium, and iron only. No forbidden sources were used. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white rice (Asian preparation) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. Glycemic load per serving (350 g) calculated as (72 × 46.9) / 100 = 33.8, reported as 33.9. The input dietary_tags included 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' which are incorrect for a dish containing fish; these tags have been removed. is_halal set to true as the dish is consistent with halal preparation at Singapore hawker centres, though individual stall certification should be verified. Ontology codes are approximate; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry exists for Singapore economic rice as of 2024.
